rules.risk: Processes inside the container execute as UID 0. If the container shares the host user namespace, a breakout grants immediate host root access.
rules.remediation: Set a non-root user in the OCI runtime spec (process.user.uid) or use --user flag at container creation.

rules.risk: CAP_SYS_ADMIN enables mount, namespace, and cgroup operations that can bypass container isolation. Effectively equivalent to privileged mode.
rules.remediation: Remove CAP_SYS_ADMIN from the capability bounding set. Use fine-grained capabilities instead.

rules.description: Bind mounts exist that are not readonly
rules.risk: Writable bind mounts allow the container to modify host files. An attacker can tamper with host binaries, cron jobs, or configuration files.
rules.remediation: Add 'ro' to mount options for all bind mounts that do not require write access.

rules.risk: Without a seccomp profile, the container can invoke any system call. Dangerous syscalls (e.g., mount, ptrace, reboot) remain available to attackers.
rules.remediation: Apply a seccomp profile via --security-opt seccomp=<profile.json> or set seccompProfilePath in the OCI runtime spec.

rules.description: Systemd service unit does not set a non-root User directive
rules.risk: The container engine process runs as UID 0 on the host. Combined with a container escape, this yields immediate root access to the host.
rules.remediation: Add 'User=<non-root>' to the [Service] section of the systemd unit file.

rules.description: Readonly root filesystem is not enabled
rules.risk: An attacker who gains code execution inside the container can write to the root filesystem, enabling persistence or binary replacement.
rules.remediation: Set root.readonly to true in the OCI runtime spec or use --read-only flag.
